question_text: A power-driven vessel is crossing a Traffic Separation Scheme. Under IRPCS Rule 10, the vessel should normally cross the traffic lanes:
option_b: At right angles to the general direction of traffic flow
option_c: Parallel to the lane before turning across
option_d: Only after obtaining permission from the coastal state

question_text: While following a passage plan, repeated fixes show the vessel is being set to starboard by a tidal stream. The best immediate response is to:
option_a: Continue as planned until the next waypoint is reached
option_b: Alter course and/or speed as required, then monitor the effect with fresh fixes
option_c: Ignore the set if the gyro compass is steady
option_d: Reduce speed to dead slow ahead and wait for slack water

question_text: Which charted objects are generally most suitable for visual position fixing?
option_a: Objects that are conspicuous, accurately charted, and give well-separated position lines
option_b: Any objects close to the ship, even if uncharted
option_d: Only radar conspicuous wrecks

question_text: On a chart, the light characteristic 'Fl(3) 10s' means:
option_a: Three flashes in total, then the light is extinguished permanently after 10 seconds
option_b: A flashing light showing a group of three flashes every 10 seconds
option_c: A fixed light visible for 3 seconds every 10 seconds
option_d: Three different coloured flashes visible within 10 miles

question_text: A vessel has 240 nautical miles to run and expects to maintain 12 knots. Ignoring time-zone changes, the time required is:
option_b: 12 hours
option_d: 20 hours

question_text: When planning landfall, which factor is MOST critical?
option_a: The paint scheme of the lighthouse
option_b: The expected set and drift, visibility, and the reliability of the available fixing marks
option_c: Whether the vessel can arrive exactly at civil twilight
option_d: The number of other vessels anchored offshore

question_text: To obtain the chart datum depth from an echo sounder reading, the navigator should normally:
option_a: Add the height of tide above chart datum to the observed depth
option_b: Subtract the height of tide above chart datum from the observed depth corrected to the waterline or keel datum used
option_c: Ignore the tide if under-keel clearance is adequate
option_d: Multiply the observed depth by the tidal range fraction

question_text: At anchor, the clearest early indication that a vessel may be dragging is:
option_b: The cable leading consistently in one direction with the vessel maintaining a fixed bearing to shore objects
option_c: Changing bearings or GPS position showing movement relative to fixed reference points
option_d: A small change in engine-room temperature

question_text: TPC is the number of tonnes required to:
option_b: Change the mean draught by 1 centimetre
option_d: Flood one compartment by 1 centimetre

question_text: Fresh Water Allowance (FWA) is the change in draught when a vessel moves:
option_a: From fresh water to dock water at the same displacement
option_b: From salt water to fresh water at the same displacement
option_c: From ballast condition to loaded condition
option_d: From summer load line to winter load line

question_text: A vessel must increase her mean draught by 15 cm. If TPC is 20 tonnes, the weight to load is:
option_b: 150 tonnes
option_d: 1,333 tonnes

question_text: Dock Water Allowance (DWA) is used mainly to correct for:
option_b: The effect of water density different from standard sea water on draught
option_c: The effect of free surface on GM
option_d: The increase of trim due to hogging

question_text: The righting moment at a particular angle of heel is found from:
option_b: KM minus KG
option_d: MCTC multiplied by trim

question_text: A stiff vessel is most likely to have:
option_a: A long slow roll and small GM
option_b: A quick short roll and relatively large GM
option_c: No righting lever at small angles
option_d: A permanent angle of loll in calm water

question_text: Which statement correctly distinguishes list from loll?
option_a: List is caused by off-centre weights; loll is associated with negative or very small initial GM
option_b: List is caused only by wind; loll is caused only by current
option_c: List and loll are identical terms
option_d: Loll occurs only in dry dock

question_text: Free surface effect in a slack tank will normally:
option_b: Reduce effective GM
option_c: Have no effect unless the ship is aground
option_d: Increase displacement without changing stability

question_text: If a weight is shifted transversely to starboard, the vessel will:
option_b: List to starboard
option_d: Rise bodily in the water

question_text: The angle of vanishing stability is the angle at which:
option_b: The rudder becomes ineffective
option_c: The righting lever becomes zero and positive stability is lost beyond that point
option_d: The vessel always capsizes immediately

question_text: A trimming moment is calculated as:
option_a: Weight multiplied by longitudinal distance moved
option_b: Displacement multiplied by GM
option_d: LCB minus LCG

question_text: Loading a weight well forward of the LCF will tend to make the vessel:
option_b: Trim by the stern
option_d: Remain on even keel regardless of weight

question_text: MCTC is the moment required to:
option_b: Change mean draught by 1 centimetre
option_d: Change the metacentric height by 1 metre

question_text: A vessel has draughts F 8.2 m and A 8.8 m and is assumed to be free from hog or sag. The mean draught is:
A vessel on even keel is one that:
option_b: Has equal draught forward and aft
option_d: Must be in fresh water

question_text: Using cargo gear to raise a heavy load clear of the deck will normally:
option_b: Raise the effective centre of gravity and reduce GM
option_c: Have no stability effect until the load is landed ashore
option_d: Increase MCTC only

question_text: When fixing a ship's position by radar ranges and bearings, the most reliable targets are usually:
option_b: Prominent charted objects with clear radar definition
option_d: Any return near the ship regardless of charted identity

question_text: A compass error determined by azimuth is the angle between:
option_a: The ship's head and the wake
option_b: Compass north and true north
option_c: Magnetic north and grid north only
option_d: The vessel's heading and the nearest waypoint

question_text: In restricted visibility, the action that best supports safe navigation is to:
option_b: Maintain a proper lookout, use radar systematically, and proceed at a safe speed
option_c: Continue at normal service speed if on schedule
option_d: Switch off bridge alarms to reduce distraction

question_text: Which publication is primarily used to check the current status of lights, buoys, and other navigational warnings for a voyage?
option_b: Notices to Mariners and relevant navigational warnings
option_d: Statement of facts

question_text: When calculating tidal height by the rule of twelfths, the method is suitable mainly for:
option_b: A rough estimate where a simple approximation is acceptable
option_d: Currents but not heights

question_text: The chief purpose of parallel indexing on radar is to:
option_a: Measure the ship's speed through the water
option_b: Monitor the vessel's track relative to a hazard or planned line
option_d: Replace all visual fixing methods at night

question_text: If a vessel is overtaking another vessel, the overtaking vessel must keep clear until:
option_b: The overtaken vessel sounds one short blast
option_c: She is finally past and clear
option_d: Sunrise the next day

question_text: Which condition most commonly leads to an error in gyrocompass indication at high latitude?
option_b: Latitude and speed error effects
option_d: Echo sounder lag only

question_text: A valid reason for amending a passage plan during a voyage is:
option_a: The bridge team prefers a shorter watch
option_b: New navigational information or changing conditions make revision necessary
option_c: The cook requests an earlier arrival time
option_d: The chart folio is too large to handle easily

question_text: Before entering a coastal pilotage area, the bridge team should place special emphasis on:
option_a: Reducing all written records to save time
option_b: Master-pilot exchange, expected courses and speeds, and no-go areas
option_d: Keeping all engine-room doors open

question_text: If slack tank free surface is reducing stability, the most effective general corrective action is to:
option_a: Create more slack tanks to spread the liquid
option_b: Press up or empty the affected slack tanks where operationally possible
option_d: Shift weight higher to improve rolling comfort
